vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's lcredit parameter controls requirements for usage of lowercase letters in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many lowercase characters. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each lowercase character. Modify the lcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of a lowercase character in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_lcredit='' if grep -sq lcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/lcredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^lcredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_lcredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage_decision.reason: Changing the PAM lcredit setting to require at least one lowercase character is a standard password policy hardening measure that does not affect core authentication services or system stability.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if existing passwords lack lowercase characters

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We must write a plan, then call review_plan. According to workflow: first respond with plan. Then call review_plan. After approval, we must modify /etc/security/pwquality.conf (or .conf.d) to set lcredit to -1? Actually need to ensure at least one lowercase character. The lcredit parameter: negative number -> requires that many lowercase characters. So -1 means at least one lowercase. Actually the docs: -1 means require one lowercase character. So we should set lcredit=-1.

Check whether existing /etc/security/pwquality.conf or .conf files contain lcredit. We need to read file(s). On Rocky Linux, pwquality config usually /etc/security/pwquality.conf. So read that file. If lcredit line exists, modify. If not, append. Use sed -i 's/^#?lcredit\s*=.*$/lcredit = -1/' to modify. If not found, append 'lcredit = -1' to file.

We must do it with minimal steps.

So plan: Read /etc/security/pwquality.conf. Use sed to modify or append. Then optionally check /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf too, but rule likely expects main file. Typically it's fine to modify main.

So produce plan description. Then call review_plan.

execution_details.stdout:
# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits
# Defaults:
#
# Number of characters in the new password that must not be present in the
# old password.
difok = 1
#
# Minimum acceptable size for the new password (plus one if
# credits are not disabled which is the default). (See pam_cracklib manual.)
# Cannot be set to lower value than 6.
# minlen = 8
#
# The maximum credit for having digits in the new password. If less than 0
# it is the minimum number of digits in the new password.
dcredit = -1
#
# The maximum credit for having uppercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of uppercase characters in the new
# password.
# ucredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having lowercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of lowercase characters in the new
# password.
# lcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having other characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of other characters in the new
# password.
# ocredit = 0
#
# The minimum number of required classes of characters for the new
# password (digits, uppercase, lowercase, others).
# minclass = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive same characters in the new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxrepeat = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive characters of the same class in the
# new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxclassrepeat = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the passwd entry GECOS string of the user.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# gecoscheck = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the cracklib dictionary.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
dictcheck = 1
#
# Whether to check if it contains the user name in some form.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# usercheck = 1
#
# Length of substrings from the username to check for in the password
# The check is enabled if the value is greater than 0 and usercheck is enabled.
# usersubstr = 0
#
# Whether the check is enforced by the PAM module and possibly other
# applications.
# The new password is rejected if it fails the check and the value is not 0.
# enforcing = 1
#
# Path to the cracklib dictionaries. Default is to use the cracklib default.
# dictpath =
#
# Prompt user at most N times before returning with error. The default is 1.
# retry = 3
#
# Enforces pwquality checks on the root user password.
# Enabled if the option is present.
#
# Skip testing the password quality for users that are not present in the
# /etc/passwd file.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# local_users_only

# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits

llm_verdict.message: The lcredit setting in `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` has been updated to enforce at least one lowercase character in passwords. No fragment files were present, so no additional changes were required. All remediation steps completed successfully.

Steps: read_file /etc/security/pwquality.conf to see if lcredit is present. If it's commented, uncomment and set -1. If not present, add line. Also check html files for /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf. But maybe simpler: target standard location /etc/security/pwquality.conf only. If there are config fragments, we should be consistent. But we should modify only /etc/security/pwquality.conf. But we might also need to ensure there is no override.

The plan: Read files to ensure no present conflicting config. Then modify.

Should use sed -i to modify. Do not duplicate lines.

We need to avoid interfering with other config lines.

Check the file content. If lcredit is commented: '# lcredit = ...', we uncomment and set to -1. If lcredit present: use sed to replace value. If not present: append line 'lcredit = -1'

We'll do those steps.

So propose to use read_file to inspect file, but we don't run commands yet.

Approach: plan:

1. Read /etc/security/pwquality.conf using read_file. Check for pattern ^\s*#?\s*lcredit\s*=.*. If present in a single line, modify with sed: e.g. sed -E -i 's/^#?\\s*lcredit\\s*=.*$/lcredit = -1/' . If not found, append new line.

2. Also check /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf for lcredit entries. If any exist, modify similarly. (But to keep minimal, we can skip unless necessary. But we can mention optional further steps to ensure override is removed.)

Given the developer instructions: "Do NOT duplicate config lines. Use sed -i to modify existing values in-place." So we can use sed to modify lines. We need single commands. But no exec yet.

Thus the plan description should enumerate files to modify and commands. Since we cannot chain commands, we can list each as a separate command. But plan description is just textual. Then we will later run commands one by one. So the plan description should include what commands will be executed. Then review_plan will evaluate.

Let's produce plan description string with precise plan. Then call review_plan.

Now produce the tool call:

{
"plan_description": "Detailed plan..."
}

We need to ensure JSON is valid. We'll produce a JSON with correct syntax.

Let's draft plan description:

"Plan: The goal is to enforce a minimum of one lowercase character in passwords by setting lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf (and any fragment files). Steps: 1) Read /etc/security/pwquality.conf via read_file to confirm existing lcredit line. 2) If a line containing lcredit exists (whether commented or active) use sed -i to replace the line with 'lcredit = -1'. Command: sed -E -i 's/^[#\\s]*lcredit\\s*=.*$/lcredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf. 3) If no such line, append 'lcredit = -1' to the file using sed -i -e '$a\\lcredit = -1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf. 4) Scan all fragment files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf similarly: For each file matching, if it contains a line with lcredit, replace it; else append. Commands: sed -E -i 's/^[#\\s]*lcredit\\s*=.*$/lcredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; and if no match, sed -i -e '$a\\lcredit = -1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf. 5) No other modifications. End plan."

But we need to be careful with quoting inside JSON. We need to escape backslashes properly. Also avoid misuse of shell syntax. But we can describe the commands in plain. For JSON, we need to escape quotes.

We can incorporate the commands inside the plan description, but not actual execution. That is fine.

We need to comply with developer instructions: "One command at a time. Do NOT chain." So plan description is not actual execution. It's just description.

All good.

Where "..." is the plan.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Review output was not valid JSON. Raw: {"finding_id":"accounts_password_pam_lcredit","is_optimal":false,"approve":true,"feedback":"The proposed steps effectively enforce the required lowercase policy and would address the vulnerability if ...

The plan correctly addresses the vulnerability by enforcing a lowercase character requirement through lcredit = -1 in both the main pwquality.conf and any fragment files. The approach is straightforward and preserves existing configurations. A few minor adjustments can improve robustness and cleanliness.
concerns: Potential error if no fragment files exist, as the sed commands may fail or produce warnings.
The append command uses a double backslash (\) which could inadvertently add a literal backslash to the file.
The sed replacement pattern will remove any comment or trailing comments on the lcredit line, which may be acceptable but could alter existing comments.
The plan may leave duplicate lcredit entries across multiple fragment files, which is redundant but not harmful.
suggested_improvements: Check for the existence of fragment files before applying sed; skip the command if none exist or use a nullglob to avoid shell errors.
Use a safer append syntax such as `sed -i -e '$a lcredit = -1'` to avoid unintended backslashes.
Consider consolidating the lcredit setting into a single config file if multiple fragment files are used, to avoid redundancy.
Add a comment line before the new lcredit entry to indicate why it was added for audit purposes.
